text: Hello, I'm sharing a case of a very symptomatic high epigastric incisional hernia. It was from a mediastinal tube site from a prior sternotomy.
The surgeon observes incarcerated omentum within the hernia sac, displaying signs of fat necrosis and erythema on the overlying skin.
And so he presents for repair.
The video shows the previously placed mesh from a right inguinal hernia repair in good position. A small left inguinal hernia is also visible, though it appears asymptomatic.
The surgeon places robotic ports a few centimeters below the umbilicus on the right, middle, and left, and proceeds to dock the robotic system.
The surgeon reduces the hernia contents, with the omentum visibly breaking during the process.
The surgeon observes a significant amount of necrotic omental tissue within the hernia sac, which will require further debridement.
The surgeon begins creating a flap just above the level of the umbilicus to perform a Transabdominal Preperitoneal (TAPP) hernia repair, avoiding the retro rectus approach.

And my thoughts were just to get enough coverage for this, you know, maybe two, two and a half centimeter epigastric incisional defect. But you'll see as I get a little bit higher, I found something that clinically on exam I didn't know about.

And I had not gotten a CAT scan on the patient prior to surgery. And so right now, I'm just thinking, okay, I'm going to make a flap. I'm going to close my defect, put in some mesh. But I find something that altered my plans a little bit.
The surgeon dissects above the preperitoneal midline fat, aiming to reach the anterior abdominal wall and posterior sheath. The peritoneum appears intact, facilitating the dissection process.

Initially for the small defect, I did not think that I needed to go too wide for the repair, but with what I ended up finding after, I actually had to extend it. It's important in these cases to make the mouth of the flap pretty wide.

The surgeon continues the preperitoneal dissection, moving cephalad while widening the dissected space to prevent tearing of the flap by the lateral edges as the right and left arms of the instruments navigate the area.

The surgeon observes the hernia defect and instructs the bedside assistant to apply pressure on it. The tissue appears necrotic and fibrotic, preventing further protrusion. The video does not show the debridement process from the intra-abdominal location.
The surgeon dissects the left and right sides of the hernia defect, aiming to elevate the tissue towards the subxiphoid area, creating a volcano-like appearance.
The surgeon identifies the mediastinal tube incisional hernia, achieving the 'volcano sign' by dissecting around the hernia defect.

The surgeon attempts to reduce the hernia sac, but due to extensive scarring and fat necrosis, the sac is amputated, leaving a small defect the size of the hernia neck. This defect is visible and will be closed later in the procedure.

The surgeon observes omentum and necrotic fat descending with the hernia sac. They aim to create cephalic coverage of approximately four to five centimeters for mesh placement. The xiphoid process is identified, and the surgeon attempts to create space beneath it for the mesh, discovering a previously unnoticed xiphoid defect.

The surgeon has uncovered and reduced necrotic fat from a defect in the hernia site. To prevent the development of an asymptomatic hernia, the surgeon extends the flap superiorly and prepares to use a larger mesh for repair.
The surgeon performs a subxiphoid dissection, revealing the split xiphoid bone, which was affected during the sternotomy and either left unclosed or reopened post-surgery.

The surgeon identifies small defects on the left and right of the xiphoid, resembling a Morgagni hernia. The fat is reduced from the right side defect during the subxiphoid dissection, which is extended across the midline. A similar defect is visible on the left side, and the surgeon plans to cover these areas with mesh.

The surgeon carefully dissects along the peritoneum, ensuring no muscle is posterior to the dissection. Due to the patient's anticoagulation, meticulous hemostasis is maintained. The surgeon creates additional space superiorly underneath the diaphragm for the mesh placement.
The surgeon selects a 15 by 15 cm mesh for the hernia repair but realizes a larger mesh is needed due to the uncovered xiphoid defect and additional space requirements.
The surgeon performs central cephalad dissection, measuring the pocket for mesh placement. A Versatex mesh is cut to dimensions of twenty by seventeen centimeters for use in the repair.

The surgeon attempts to close the subxiphoid or xiphoid sternal defect, ensuring the mesh can sit beneath it with adequate tissue coverage. The needle is maneuvered along the periosteum of the bone and connective tissue without penetrating the bone.

The surgeon positions the mesh over the defect, ensuring it covers the area adequately to prevent eventration into the defect during the early post-operative period. The mesh is placed to integrate with surrounding tissue, providing coverage over the open bone area.
The surgeon decides to use a single large piece of Versatex mesh instead of two separate pieces, preparing to place it over the hernia defect.
The surgeon closes the epigastric incisional defect in a transverse orientation, with the tissue edges coming together easily due to the small size of the defect.
The surgeon runs the needle back through the tissue for additional security, displaying the twenty by seventeen Versatex mesh being used in the procedure.

The surgeon uses a trio silk suture at the cranial aspect of the mesh to secure it beneath the sternum against the diaphragm, preventing it from sliding out. Another suture is placed at the distal aspect of the mesh, with additional cardinal sutures applied.

The surgeon closes the large wide mouth flaps in the middle, moving towards the patient's right side. Meticulous hemostasis is achieved to prevent bleeding, especially since the patient is anticoagulated.

The surgeon completes the closure of the left side of the flap using a nine-inch, 3-0 V-Loc suture, running it down to close the defect where the hernia sac was amputated. Small peritoneal rents are also closed using Vicryl sutures.

But overall, I feel like the case went well. The patient was discharged from the recovery room. so far is doing well um i'm interested in comments and suggestions if anybody had anything else that they think would have been good to do thank you for watching
